title: Functional methods for quantum thermodynamics
authors: Sibo Wang, Samuel Degen, Haozhao Liang
abstract: The functional renormalization group provides a nonperturbative and systematically improvable route to constructing density functionals for quantum many-body systems from microscopic Hamiltonians. Here we advance this program by benchmarking functional-renormalization-group density functional theory (FRG-DFT) against the exact thermodynamics of the single-site Bose-Hubbard model. This model provides an ideal testing ground because it is analytically solvable, yet remains subtle in the imaginary-time coherent-state path integral, where a naive continuum treatment generates a spurious self-interaction. We show that a careful Hubbard-Stratonovich derivation identifies the self-interaction correction term that must be included in the FRG-DFT flow to recover the exact thermodynamics. We then systematically compare several closures of the resulting hierarchy of flow equations for the free energy, chemical potential, and connected density correlators over broad ranges of density, temperature, and interaction strength. The benchmark shows that the free energy is comparatively robust, whereas the chemical potential and fluctuation observables provide much sharper diagnostics of the hierarchy closure. A maximum-entropy closure gives the most accurate overall description and reproduces even the low-temperature oscillatory structure of the connected two-density correlator. These results identify two general requirements for functional approaches to quantum thermodynamics: the renormalization group flow equation must retain the equal-time contact subtraction to avoid spurious self-interactions, and any closure of the hierarchy must preserve the statistical consistency of density correlators. This work provides a controlled foundation for deriving ab initio density functionals for quantum many-body systems across condensed-matter, ultracold-atom, and nuclear physics, as well as quantum chemistry.
summary: This work advances Functional Renormalization Group Density Functional Theory (FRG-DFT) to quantum thermodynamics by benchmarking it against the exactly solvable single-site Bose-Hubbard model. The authors identify that a self-interaction correction term is necessary for formal consistency, and they demonstrate that the maximum-entropy closure scheme provides the most accurate description of thermodynamic observables and density correlations. This establishes a controlled foundation for deriving quantum many-body functionals.
main_problem: The paper aims to develop a systematic and controllable functional route using Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) to derive energy functionals for quantum many-body systems in the context of quantum thermodynamics.
main_result: The maximum-entropy closure scheme (Scheme IV) is shown to be the most accurate, successfully reproducing exact thermodynamic quantities and the low-temperature oscillatory structure of density correlators.
method: The core method is FRG-DFT, which involves deriving flow equations for the effective action by systematically truncating an infinite hierarchy of equations derived from the imaginary-time path integral.
model_or_system: The primary testbed is the single-site Bose-Hubbard model, which is analytically solvable and allows for rigorous benchmarking against exact finite-temperature thermodynamics.
key_observables: Free energy, chemical potential, and connected density correlators ($	ilde{G}^{(2)}, 	ilde{G}^{(3)}, 	ilde{G}^{(4)}$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), interaction strength ($g$), and particle density ($ho$ or $N$). The low-temperature (strong-coupling) regime is a key diagnostic test.
main_assumptions: The derivation requires a careful Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation and the inclusion of a Self-Interaction Correction (SIC) term to maintain formal consistency. The analysis relies on comparing results across various closure approximations.
figures_summary: Figures compare the flow evolution of $	ilde{G}^{(2)}$ and other correlators across different closure schemes (I, II, III, IV), demonstrating that Scheme IV best reproduces exact results, especially the oscillatory structure at low temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the general FRG-DFT formalism, then applies it to the single-site Bose-Hubbard model, deriving the necessary SIC term. It then systematically compares four different truncation/closure schemes (I-IV) by benchmarking their predictions for thermodynamic observables against exact solutions.
why_interesting: The rigorous development of FRG-DFT provides a powerful, non-perturbative tool for calculating quantum thermodynamic properties, offering a systematic path to derive ab initio functionals applicable across condensed matter, ultracold atoms, and nuclear physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Flow evolution for scheme I (minimal closure) at T/g = 1.0 and N = 5.0. Panels (a)–(c) show the running free energy per particle, chemical potential, and connected two-density correlator as functions of λ, respectively. Dashed and solid lines denote the naive and SIC formulations, respectively. The open circle marks the exact result in the fully interacting theory at λ = 1.
FIG. 2. Equation of state from scheme I (minimal closure) with SIC at three different temperatures T/g. Panels (a) and (b) show ¯E/g and g ˜G(2), respectively, compared with the exact thermodynamics.
FIG. 3. The same as Fig. 1, but for the truncation scheme II (frozen closure).
FIG. 4. The same as Fig. 1, but for the truncation scheme III (effective occupation closure).
FIG. 5. The same as Fig. 1, but for the truncation scheme IV (maximum-entropy closure).
